coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-sulfur co-doped pH-insensitive fluorescent carbon dots for high sensitive and selective hypochlorite detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Liyang
Zhou, Guohua
Xiang, Xia
Zhang, Zhen
Jia, Yongmei
Liu, Peilian
Li, Zhiguo
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterial with exceptional properties and have drawn great attention in recent years. However, the preparation and applications of high-quality carbon dots remain challenging. Here, we describe a simple hydrothermal synthesis route using citric acid as a carbon source for stable fluorescent CDs. The CDs are modified with glutathione and exhibit high fluorescent quantum yields (30.2%) and excellent photo-stability. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains stable over a wide range of pH values (3−12). Hypochlorite (ClO−) can effectively quench the fluorescence of the CDs by destroying the pyrrolic ring and conjugate structure of the CDs. Thus, the CDs can be used to detect ClO−. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity changes of CDs correspond selectively to ClO− in the range of 100–800 nmol/L with a LOD of 16 nmol/L. Practical applications of the proposed method for free chlorine detection in tap water show similar results and recovery compared to the standard DPD-based method. These results suggest that the pH-insensitive CDs prepared via this facile procedure are a promising chemosensor for free chlorine and have great potential in analytical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Free chlorine
Fluorescent detection
Hypochlorite
pH

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ClO− with CD probe

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs synthesized from different reactants. All reactants were maintained at the same molar ratio; CDs were excited at 340 nm. The influence of (b) the mass ratio of reactants, (c) the reaction time, and (d) the temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CDs was studied.

(a) TEM image of CDs. (b) Sizes of CD particles calculated from TEM images. (c) High-resolution TEM image of CD particles. (d) FTIR spectra of CDs, GSH and citric acid. (e) The XPS survey scan of the CDs, and high-resolution (f) C1s, (g) O1s, (h) N1s, and (i) S2p XPS spectra of CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths of CDs in aqueous solution. (b) Normalized fluorescence spectra of CDs under various excitation wavelengths. (c) Normalized fluorescence intensity of CDs under continuous irradiation for 20 min at 340 nm. (d) Normalized fluorescence intensity of CDs at different pH and relative normalized fluorescence spectra. The pH ranges from 2.0 to 12.0 were created with Britton-Robinson buffer solutions.

(a) H−NMR spectra of CDs and CDs with ClO− for analyzing the mechanism of fluorescence quenching by ClO−. (b) The results of the optimization of anionic ions to eliminate the interference of Fe3+, (c) effect of pH, and (d) the reaction time for ClO− detection. The concentrations of anionic ions, Fe3+, and ClO− used in experiments are 0.3 mmol/L, 40 μmol/L, and 2 μmol/L, respectively.

The results show the analytical performance of CDs for ClO−. a) The fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of ClO− and b) relative standard curve of ΔI versus ClO−. c) Fluorescence responses of ClO− compared with other cations and d) anions. The concentrations of all ions used in the comparison are 2 mmol/L.
Comparison of different CD-based methods for ClO− detection.

row.entry: Citric acid, L-tartaric acid and ethanediamine

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: n.p., not presented; –, no interference in the paper.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The sensitivity is expressed as the slope of standard curve that presents the relationship between F 0-F/F 0 and concentrations, where F 0 is the fluorescence of CDs without ClO−, and F is the fluorescence of CDs after incubated with ClO−.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The data in brackets are the slopes of the standard curves for ΔI versus concentrations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis and comparison of DPD-based method and proposed method for free chlorine detection in tap water.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-sulfur co-doped pH-insensitive fluorescent carbon dots for high sensitive and selective hypochlorite detection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Product Processing and Nutriology, Oil Crops Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Hubei Key Laboratory of Lipid Chemistry and Nutrition, Ministry of Agriculture Key Laboratory of Oil Crops Biology, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterial with exceptional properties and have drawn great attention in recent years. However, the preparation and applications of high-quality carbon dots remain challenging. Here, we describe a simple hydrothermal synthesis route using citric acid as a carbon source for stable fluorescent CDs. The CDs are modified with glutathione and exhibit high fluorescent quantum yields (30.2%) and excellent photo-stability. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains stable over a wide range of pH values (3−12). Hypochlorite (ClO−) can effectively quench the fluorescence of the CDs by destroying the pyrrolic ring and conjugate structure of the CDs. Thus, the CDs can be used to detect ClO−. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity changes of CDs correspond selectively to ClO− in the range of 100–800 nmol/L with a LOD of 16 nmol/L. Practical applications of the proposed method for free chlorine detection in tap water show similar results and recovery compared to the standard DPD-based method. These results suggest that the pH-insensitive CDs prepared via this facile procedure are a promising chemosensor for free chlorine and have great potential in analytical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have many advantages over conventional heavy-metal-based and semi-conductor quantum dots. They are a shining star in biological science. Due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and easy fabrication, carbon dots have great impacts on different applications such as biosensors [1], bioimaging [2], and fluorescent probes for metal ions [3]. Sun et al. first reported a new method to prepare carbon nanoparticles via laser ablation. They named the fluorescent carbon nanomaterials smaller than 10 nm as CDs [4]. Since then, many syntheses for obtaining CDs have been discussed. However, a facile synthesis of highly fluorescent and stable CDs remains a challenge. Common routes to preparing CDs include microwave synthesis method [5], pyrolysis [6] template-assisted methods [7], and hydrothermal treatment [8]. To enhance the quantum yield (QY) of CDs, most methods adopt surface passivation reagents to modify diverse substrates and prepare functional composite materials by simple chemistry.

For example, Tan et al. reported that the QY of the CDs modified with four types of passivation ligands was obviously improved: 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA), poly-l-lysine (PLL), cysteine and chitosan [2]. In addition, heteroatom-doped CDs also show a distinguished improvement in fluorescence properties. Yang et al. reported that the quantum yield of the nitrogen-doped CDs obtained by the hydrothermal method reached up to 80% [9]. Using a similar synthesis, sulfur-doped CDs prepared by Xu et al. using sodium citrate and sodium thiosulfate as precursors also exhibited significant QY up to 67% [10]. Besides, multi-heteroatom co-doping is another effective way to improve the quality of CDs, such as nitrogen-sulfur co-doped [11,12] and nitrogen-chlorine co-doped [13].

The stability of CDs is another concern. CDs are usually photo-stable, which is an advantage in imaging applications. However, the pH stability of CDs has not been well studied. Some papers report that CDs are sensitive to pH and can be used for pH sensing [14–16]. However, pH resistance is important in many applications. Therefore, many efforts have been exerted to improve the pH stability of nanomaterials such as gold nanoparticles [17] and quantum dots [18,19]. Here, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots were prepared via a hydrothermal method using citric acid monohydrate as a carbon source and glutathione (GSH) as a modification reagent. The CDs have high quality with a high quantum yield and excellent photostability and pH stability. These are ideal properties for analytical and biological applications. Thus, CD-based selective sensing of hypochlorite (ClO−) was investigated.

ClO− is widely used as an antimicrobial agent in water treatment [20] and is implicated as a contributing factor in many pathological conditions [21,22]. According to the guideline of the World Health Organization (WHO), the residual concentration of free chlorine in drinking water should be more than 0.2 mg/L (3.9 μmol/L) for safety requirements [23]. Free chlorine mainly includes hypochloric acid and hypochlorite. Therefore, it is important to monitor the ClO− in drinking water for health purposes. Many novel technologies have been developed for the detection of ClO− [24–28]. CDs have been involved in ClO− detection as well [26–29] based on the fluorescence quenching of CDs induced by the oxidation of CDs with ClO− [26]. The oxidation ability of ClO− is pH-dependent. Thus, the CD-based detection of ClO− should be performed at low pH to improve the sensitivity. However, most of the carbon dots are sensitive to pH, and thus the reported ClO− detection is performed in weakly alkaline buffer solution [26]. This limits the sensitivity of detection. In this paper, the pH-insensitive nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs significantly improved the analysis sensitivity of ClO− with good performance for ClO− detection in drinking water.

Citric acid, glutathione (GSH), and sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Other reagents used in the experiment are analytically pure reagents unless otherwise stated.

The 0.180 g citric acid monohydrate and 0.010 g GSH were added in a 50-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and dissolved in 5 mL distilled water. The mixture was heated to 200 °C for 4 h and then cooled to room temperature. The excess citric acid was then removed by adding 0.18 g calcium carbonate (CaCO3) at 60 °C and reacted completely at 90 °C for 30 min followed by purification with gel filtration chromatography. The nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs were obtained after evaporating and drying leading to blue luminescence.

The CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI TECNAI G2 F20 200KV), infrared spectroscopy (IR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ULVAC-PHI, PHI 5000 VersaProbe with an Al–K X-ray source) to determinate the structure, surface morphology and surface active groups. The fluorescence and absorption spectra of CDs were measured on a Horiba Jobin-Yvon FluoroMax-4 Spectrofluorometer and Tecan Infinite 200 PRO, respectively.

To investigate the interaction between CDs and ClO−, solid CDs were dissolved in D2O, and then solid calcium hypochlorite was added. The H−NMR spectrum of the solution was measured with a nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker 400 MHz) after 50 min of incubation. CDs in D2O without hypochlorite were measured as a control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO− with CD probe
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of ClO− in the stock solution was calibrated via the iodine method. Briefly, 10 mL of ClO− stock solution was mixed with 10 mL KI (1 mol/L) in an iodine flask, followed by 5 mL of HCl (6 mol/L). The mixture was covered and reacted for 5 min in the dark. After incubation, 100 mL of water was added prior to titration with Na2S2O3 standard solution that had been calibrated with K2CrO7. The concentration of ClO− stock solution can be calculated according to the volume and concentration of Na2S2O3 standard solution used in the titration. The ClO− stock solution was used in the following experiments.

The CDs were diluted with phosphate buffer solution (0.1 mol/L, pH 2.5), and then standard ClO− stock solution with different concentrations or samples was added and incubated for 50 min before fluorescence measurements. A standard curve was made according to the measurements. The concentrations of ClO− in samples were determined according to the standard curve.

Citric acid was adopted as the carbon source for preparing CDs, and GSH was selected as the nitrogen and sulfur source. A yellow aqueous solution of CDs was obtained after the materials were sealed and reacted. The product could be dried to a solid. Both the aqueous and solid forms of CD emit blue fluorescence when irradiated with a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 1 ).

Although CDs prepared from citric acid by carbonization have been reported, citric acid could not form fluorescent CDs under hydrothermal conditions. GSH has reactive –SH and –NH2 and likely plays an essential role in the reaction. To validate this assumption, four reactions were carried out: citric acid alone; GSH alone; citric acid and cysteine; and citric acid and GSH. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs synthesized via these different reactants are shown in Fig. 2a. The results show that the products obtained from citric acid or GSH have no fluorescence, while the products of the other two reactions display high fluorescence. As we know, both GSH and cysteine have –SH and –NH2, which can provide sulfur and nitrogen sources to enhance the fluorescence properties of the CDs. In addition, the fluorescence of CDs modified with GSH is significantly higher than the cysteine-modified one. This may be due to the higher nitrogen content of GSH versus cysteine. Thus, the results indicate that the significant improvement in CDs' fluorescence properties is the result of nitrogen and sulfur co-doping.

The synthetic conditions were optimized to improve fluorescence including the mass ratio of citric acid and GSH, the reaction time, and the temperature of the hydrothermal treatment. The mass ratio of reactants was varied by altering the mass of citric acid. The fluorescence intensity of CDs increases with the mass ratio of reactants and reaches a maximum when the mass ratio is 18:1 (Fig. 2b). Further increasing citric acid does not lead to obvious influence on the fluorescence of CDs. This occurs when the mass of citric acid increases to 0.180 g. At this point, the reaction between reactants is complete, and the optimal mass ratio of citric acid to GSH is 18:1.

The reaction times were 60, 120, 180, 240, 300, and 360 min, and the temperatures were 140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C, and 220 °C. The results show that the fluorescence intensity gradually increases to a maximum value with increasing reaction time to 240 min followed by a decrease with further reaction times (Fig. 2c). A similar tendency was observed in temperature research. Fig. 2d shows that the optimized temperature for the hydrothermal process is 200 °C. Fluorescence decreases at 220 °C. Therefore, the optimal synthetic conditions for CDs are 0.180 g citric acid, 0.010 g GSH and 240 min hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C.

The CDs synthesized under optimal conditions were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), infrared spectroscopy (IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The TEM image of CDs (Fig. 3a) shows that carbon nanoparticles are highly monodisperse and uniform with a spherical shape with a mean size of 2.5 nm (Fig. 3b). The lattice distances observed in the high-resolution TEM image of a single particle are 0.20 and 0.17 nm (Fig. 3c), which are consistent with the lattice spacing of graphite 2H (101 and 004 facets, respectively). This result suggests that the particles are high crystallinity carbon materials. The IR spectra determined the functional groups on CDs. Fig. 3d shows detailed information on the IR spectra of CDs. Compared with the IR spectra of citric acid and GSH, the spectral difference indicates the formation of CDs. There is a broad and strong band at 3409 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH and –NH2. The peaks at 1704 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 are ascribed to CO stretching vibration of –COOH and –CONH–, respectively, indicating that the CDs were covered with carboxyl groups. The peak at 1566 cm−1 appears because of the vibration of the CC bond, while that at 1432 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CN. In addition, the vibration of the CO bond produces moderate absorption at 1216 cm−1 . Therefore, the results were preliminarily proved that the as-prepared CDs are modified with carboxyl groups (–COOH) and amino groups (−NH2).

More characterization was conducted with XPS to further confirm the surface modification and element composition of as-prepared CDs. In addition to the Ca impurities introduced in the separation process, the XPS spectrum of CDs presents three obvious peaks at 284.8, 401.0, and 531.5 eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 3e). This confirms that these CDs are mainly composed of C (60.71%), N (3.57%), and O (35.09%). The binding energy of S in the S2p XPS spectrum of CDs was at 163.3 eV. This demonstrates that the CDs contain limited amount of S element (0.63%), which comes from the sulfhydryl group of GSH. The detailed C1s spectrum (Fig. 3f) shows four peaks at 284.8, 285.6, 286.4, and 288.4 eV, which are attributed to C-C/C=C, CS, CN and CO bonds, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3g) can be resolved as follows: CO at 531.4 eV and C-O-C/C-OH at 532.1 eV. All of these data validate the presence of the carboxyl group on the CDs. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3h) exhibits three peaks at 400.0, 400.5, and 401.5 eV. These are attributed to C-N-C, CN, and NH bonds. These peaks indicate that the nitro element on CDs is present as both pyrrolic type (400.0 eV) and amino type (401.5 eV) [14]. The S2p spectrum consists of two peaks centered at 163.6 and 168.8 eV suggesting that S exists in two forms (Fig. 3i). The peak located at 163.6 eV can be deconvoluted into 163.1 and 164.3 eV, which are 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of thiophene-S due to spin–orbit coupling, respectively. The latter peak can be fit with two components at 168.3 and 169.5 eV, which are consistent with 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of oxide-sulfur [12,30,31]. Therefore, the doped sulfur exists in two configurations: one is thiophene-sulfur and the other is oxide-sulfur. The XPS spectrum confirms that the CDs are doped with N and S elements–this further demonstrates the presence of the groups suggested by IR results. These results confirm our hypothesis that the strong fluorescence of CDs is a result of doping by N and S, which changes the CD surface states [32].

The luminescence of CDs is critical. Fig. 4a shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CD aqueous solution. There is an absorption peak centered at 355 nm. This is the transition caused by the surface state of CDs, which leads to strong fluorescence [11,14]. The excitation spectrum of CDs (λem = 430 nm) with a peak centered at 340 nm also confirms this conclusion. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were measured with the excitation wavelengths set from 300 to 400 nm at 20-nm increments. These values suggested that the fluorescence emission of CDs depends on the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4a), which has also been observed in other carbon-based quantum dots [14,16,31]. The fluorescence intensity gradually increased to a maximum value with increasing excitation wavelengths of 340 nm followed by a decrease in further excitation wavelength increases. The emission peaks show a red-shift with increasing excitation wavelengths (from 415 to 489 nm). This is clearly seen in the normalized fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4b). As a result, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the as-prepared CDs are 340 and 430 nm, respectively. At maximum excitation, the quantum yield of the CDs is calculated to be 30.2% with quinine sulfate as the standard underscoring the excellent luminescence properties of the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs.

The CDs also exhibit photo and pH stabilities. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs drops slightly to about 97% at the beginning under irradiation for 20 min at 340 nm (Fig. 4c). It then slowly recovers to 98% and remains relatively stable. Fig. 4d shows the stable fluorescence of CDs against pH. When excited at 340 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is strong and stable over a wide range of pH values (3.0–12.0). Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity increased dramatically to 120% when the pH was adjusted to 2.0. Besides, the fluorescence spectra red shifted gradually to 442 nm at pH values below 5.0 (Fig. 4d). This is likely due to protonation of the sulfonic acid and carboxylic acid groups on CDs because the pKa of sulfonic acid and carboxylic acid is 0–2 [33] and 3–5, respectively. As a result, the surface states of CDs changed, and the energy level difference decreased. This excellent stability of CDs over a wide range of pH makes the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs a promising material for many fields.

The ClO− can quench the fluorescence of CDs especially under acid condition. Literary works have reported that the quenching of CDs is induced by the oxidation of CDs including the oxidation of C—OH groups to —COO− [26], and the destruction of CS and CN bonds [27]. Here, interactions between CDs and ClO− were further investigated with H−NMR. Fig. 5a shows that the CDs display several strong peaks at 6.33, 5.85, 3.39, and 2.02. Combined with the XPS analysis results, the data suggest that these belong to the protons of the pyrrolic ring on the CD surface. After being reacted with ClO−, the peak at 6.33 disappears and is accompanied by a peak at 5.38., Additionally, there is a slight shift to high field of peaks at chemical shift values below 4.0. This change indicates that ClO− destroys the pyrrolic rings and weakens the conjugate structure of CDs. Thus, the change in the surface state of CDs quenches the fluorescence.

The dramatic quenching of CDs by ClO− offers sensitive detection. The conditions were optimized prior to analysis. First, the as-prepared CDs can be quenched by Fe3+ ions as well as some reports [8,10,30]. Thus, it is necessary to eliminate the interference of Fe3+ during ClO− detection. The addition of some anionic ions can mask the mental ions in the detection. This is the best option for eliminating Fe3+ interference. Fig. 5b shows that the fluorescence of CDs with the addition of Ac−, SO4 −, or NO3 − were quenched by Fe3+. However, Fe3+ does not affect the fluorescence of CDs in the presence of H2PO4 −, which means that H2PO4 − can perfectly eliminate the interference of Fe3+ during ClO− detection. This is because of the high affinity of phosphate ions for Fe3+ through Fe—O—P bonds [34], which protect CDs from being quenched by Fe3+.

The pH was subsequently optimized because pH is related to the oxidation ability of ClO−. Considering the masking effect of H2PO4 − to Fe3+, phosphate buffer solution was chosen to maintain the pH of solution. The results show that as the pH decreases from 3.25 to 2.45, the fluorescence quenching of CDs (ΔI) caused by ClO− gradually increased and plateaued (Fig. 5c). Obviously, low pH results in high oxidation ability of ClO−, and thus there is a big ΔI between blank and samples. However, the concentration of ClO− is limited in solution. Thus, a further decrease in pH will not result in a higher ΔI. Accordingly, to obtain a high and stable ΔI for ClO− detection, 0.1 mol/L phosphate buffer solution with pH 2.5 was selected for subsequent studies.

The best reaction time for detection was also optimized. The results are shown in Fig. 5d. ΔI between blank and samples increases with time indicating that the reaction between CDs and ClO− is time-dependent. It reached the highest after 50 min. and then plateaued. Therefore, an incubation time of 50 min was selected for ClO− detection.

As described above, the best fluorescence response for ClO− detection was obtained using 0.1 mol/L phosphate buffer solution with pH 2.5 and incubated for 50 min. The performance of CDs for ClO− detection was then studied under optimized conditions. Fig. 6a shows that the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases with increasing concentration of ClO−. There is a linear relationship between ΔI and ClO−concentrations from 100 to 800 nmol/L (R2 = 0.98), and the limit of detection (LOD) for ClO− was calculated to be 16 nmol/L (3σ, n = 7). The large slope (595 ± 40/nmol∙L−1) of the standard curve indicates a high sensitivity and accuracy for quantification as well. Compared with the reported CD-based methods for ClO− (Table 1 ), the LOD of this work is lower than most other reported methods and is comparable with the best ones. Table 1 shows that good performance for ClO− detection is seen at low pH values (3.0–5.0), which indicates the importance of low pH for ClO− detection. However, our CDs are pH insensitive. Furthermore, the sensitivity expressed as the slope of this method is the best (1.33/μmol∙L−1 for F 0-F/F 0 versus concentrations, where F 0 is the fluorescence of CDs without ClO−, and F is the fluorescence of CDs after incubated with ClO−). It is about 20-fold higher than other reports. That is due to the high quantum yield and pH insensitivity of CDs. Moreover, coexisting ions in water such as Fe3+ and Hg2+ would interfere with the listed low LOD methods. No interference was seen in this study. The data show that 2 mmol/L of some common cations and anions existing in water samples did not show obvious impacts on the fluorescence of CDs while ClO− quenches the CDs about 10-fold more than the other ions (Fig. 6c and d). Even if all the tested ions were present with ClO−, the effect on the fluorescence response is negligible. This indicates the high selectivity of the proposed method along with the high sensitivity for ClO− detection in water samples.

The excellent selectivity combined with high sensitivity of the CDs to ClO− suggests that the chemosensor might be directly used to detect free chlorine in real water samples. Therefore, we further examined the practical application of free chlorine detection in tap water and compared the results with an N, N-diethy1-p-phenylenediamine (DPD)-based method, which is a standard method for free chlorine detection. The tap water obtained from our laboratory without any pretreatment was analyzed with a DPD-based method and the proposed method. This was repeated with ClO− spiking. Table 2 shows the results of determinations along with recovery values of the spiked samples. The result and recovery values are similar with relative standard deviations (RSD) less than 1.5% (n = 3). These results indicate that the proposed CD is a promising chemosensor for the detection of free chlorine in tap water with excellent accuracy, precision, and sensitivity.

In summary, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal approach. The CDs were modified with glutathione (GSH) and had an average size of 2.5 nm. The results confirm heteroatom doping. The CDs have excellent solubility, photo-stability, and pH adaptability. The quantum yield reached up to 30.2%. These properties make CDs very promising for extensive applications. The CDs selectively responded to ClO− in phosphate-buffered solutions (pH 2.5), and the mechanism was studied. A sensitive detection method for ClO− was established with a linear range of 100–800 nmol/L and LOD of 16 nmol/L. Compared with existing CD-based methods, this method has the best sensitivity and high selectivity. This is due to its excellent properties. The practical application of free chlorine detection in tap water via this method offers similar accuracy and precision as a standard DPD-based method. These results indicate that the CDs proposed here are a promising chemosensor for the detection of free chlorine in real samples.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Liyang Shi:Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Resources.Guohua Zhou:Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Supervision, Visualization, Funding acquisition.Xia Xiang:Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.Zhen Zhang:Investigation.Yongmei Jia:Visualization, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition.Peilian Liu:Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.Zhiguo Li:Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 21605074, 21705071]; the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province [grant number 2014A030310274, 2016A030310362, 2017A030310604]; Research Group of Rare Earth Resource Exploiting and Luminescent Materials [grant number 2017KCXTD022]; Guangdong University Provincial Key Platform and Major Research Projects: Characteristic Innovation Project [grant number 2017KTSCX118]; and the Natural Science Foundation of Lingnan Normal University [grant number ZL1404, ZL1611]. We thank LetPub (www.letpub.com) for its linguistic assistance during the preparation of this manuscript.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from flour and their applications for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Qin, W. Lu, A.M. Asiri, A.O. Al-Youbi, X. Sun, Microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from flour and their applications for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions, Sens. Actuator B-Chem., 184 (2013) 156-162. doi:10.1016/j.snb.2013.04.079.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced photoluminescence and characterization of multicolor carbon dots using plant soot as a carbon source
